title: 8 Hidar — The Four Heavenly Creatures & The Assembly
saints: The Four Heavenly Creatures (Arba'etu Insesa) — The Support of the Throne
The Holy Virgin Mary, Theotokos (Monthly Commemoration)
narrative:
The eighth day of Hidar is given entirely to the celestial order — to the Four Heavenly Creatures, the Arba'etu Insesa, the living ones before the Throne of God whose ceaseless liturgy is the foundation of all earthly worship. They are described in Ezekiel's inaugural vision and in the Apocalypse of John with a consistency that leaves no doubt about their office: the man, the lion, the ox, and the eagle — each bearing six wings, each full of eyes before and behind, each crying 'Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God Almighty, who was and is and is to come' without ceasing, day or night, through all eternity. The Ethiopian tradition holds these four with a veneration that goes beyond the acknowledgment of their existence to the recognition of their function: they are the Support of the Throne, the living pillars of the divine majesty, the creatures whose perpetual adoration does not merely express the holiness of God but constitutes the cosmic stability that makes all created existence possible. Without their song, the tradition implicitly suggests, the universe itself would lose its orientation. Their adoration is not one activity among many; it is the activity that underlies all others.

The theology of the Four Creatures in the Ethiopian Tewahedo tradition is inseparable from the theology of the Incarnation. The four faces — human, lion, ox, eagle — are read in the patristic tradition as the four faces of the Gospels: Matthew the man, Mark the lion, Luke the ox, John the eagle. The creatures who surround the Throne of God are thus also the creatures who encircle the Incarnate Word — the four witnesses to the one mystery, the four angles from which the single divine condescension is beheld. When the Ethiopian Church commemorates the Arba'etu Insesa, it is commemorating not only the angelic order but the entire fourfold witness to the Word made flesh, and the cosmic liturgy before the Throne is understood as continuous with and foundational for the earthly liturgy in which the same Word is made present in bread and wine. The church building oriented toward Jerusalem, the four walls enclosing the tabot, the four Gospels bound together in the sacred codex — all of these are participations in the same fourfold cosmic reality that the Creatures embody.

The monthly commemoration of the Virgin Mary on this same day is not incidental — it is the Tewahedo tradition's most precise theological juxtaposition. If the Four Creatures are the Support of the Throne in the celestial order, the Theotokos is the Throne itself in the terrestrial order — the woman who bore in her womb the One who is worshipped by the Creatures without ceasing, the human being who became the living seat of the One before whom the highest angels veil their faces. The Tewahedo tradition names Mary the Ark of the New Covenant, the Holy of Holies, the Throne of the Most High — and placing her feast beside the feast of the Arba'etu Insesa is the calendar's theological declaration that earth and heaven share the same center. The Creatures worship before the Throne; Mary bore the One who fills it. Their adoration and her fiat are two expressions of the same cosmic assent.

St. Moses the Black arrives in this celestial assembly as the figure of the human creature at its most redeemed — the man who was furthest from the divine throne by the measure of his former life, and who arrived nearest to it by the measure of his subsequent holiness. The adoration of the Arba'etu Insesa is the eternal worship of beings who have never turned away; the adoration of Moses the Black is the restored worship of a being who turned away completely and returned completely, and whose return — precisely because of its completeness — makes visible what the Creatures' eternal constancy demonstrates in a different register. On the eighth day of Hidar, the celestial and the redeemed-human forms of worship stand together as a single testimony: the Throne that the Creatures support and the Theotokos bears is accessible, through the mercy that Moses found, to every human creature who turns toward it.
